content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 237 • SACRAMENTO, 95814 • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance/Administration                 Executive Director                Enforcement/Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660                                      3:2-5661                         322-6441

April 21, 1976

Mr. Robert H. James
Sachse and James
Attorneys at Law
Post Office Box 427
1092 South Main Street
Fallbrook, CA. 92028

Re: Request for Opinion
No. 76-047

Dear Mr. James:

Thank you for your letter of March 31, 1976, requesting
the Fair Political Practices Commission to issue an opinion
concerning a possible conflict of interest in the Fallbrook
Fire Protection District. Since your letter raises no substantive question involving an interpretation of the Act, no formal
opinion will be issued. However, I hope the following informal
comments will enable you to resolve the matter.

This Agency does not enforce the Moscone Governmental
Conflicts of Interest Act. Indeed, the provisions of that law
have been rendered inoperative by the passage of the Political
Reform Act. The basic conflict of interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act are found in Gov. Code §§87100 and 87103
which state:

87100. No public official at any level of state or
local government shall make, participate in making
or in any way attempt to use his official position
to influence a governmental decision in which he knows
or has reason to know he has a financial interest.

87103. An official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its
effect on the public generally, on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public official
has a direct or indirect investment worth more than
one thousand dollars ($1,000);

P20030

76-047-115

Robert James
April 24, 1976
P.O. Box Two

(b) Any real property in which the public official
has a direct or indirect interest worth more than one
thousand dollars ($1,000);

(c) Any source of income, other than loans by a
commercial lending institution in the regular course
of business, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars
($250) or more in value received by or promised to
the public official within twelve months prior to the
time when the decision is made; or

(d) Any business entity in which the public official
is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee,
or holds any position of management.

For purposes of this section, indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest
owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public
official, by an agent on behalf of a public official,
by any business entity controlled by the public official
or by a trust in which he has a substantial interest.
A business entity is controlled by a public official
if the public official, his agents, spouse and dependent
children hold more than fifty percent of the ownership
interest in the entity. A public official has a substantial interest in a trust when the official, his
spouse and dependent children have a present or future
interest worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000).

"Income" is defined in Gov. Code §82030. Section 82030(b)
provides that:

(b) "Income" does not include:

...(2) Salary and reimbursement for expenses or per
diem received from a state or local government agency ....

Hence, although the result is somewhat anomalous, the
monies received by Director Waltz from the Fire District are
not "income" within the meaning of the Political Reform Act, and
he, therefore, is not required to abstain from making decisions
affecting this income. I hasten to add, however, that this informal advice applies only to Director Waltz's duties under the

Robert James
April 21, 1976
Page Three


Political Reform Act and should not be construed as indicating
an opinion concerning the propriety of the cited actions under
any other provisions of law or under the District's incompatible
activities statement.

     If you have any further questions, please contact Kenneth
Goshorn, an attorney in the Conflicts of Interest Division, at
(916).322-6441.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Daniel H. Lowenstein
                                        Chairman

DHL:pvp

SACHSE & JAMES
ATTORNEYS AT LAW
[illegible]
[illegible]
FAIRFIELD, CALIFORNIA [illegible]

March 31, 1976

Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

SUBJECT: Request for Opinion on behalf of Joseph R. Waltz,
Jr., Board Member, Board of Directors, Fallbrook Fire
Protection District.

RE: Commission Regulation 18320 and Government Code
Section 83114.

Gentlemen:

As the attorney for the Fallbrook Fire Protection District,
Fallbrook, California, I have been directed to request a
formal opinion of your Commission in the case of Director
Joseph R. Waltz, Jr.

The Board of Directors and director Waltz are concerned that
Director Waltz may unknowingly be involved in a conflict of
interest under the Moscone Conflict of Interest
and Disclosure Act of 1973; the Act subject to interpreta-
tion and enforcement by your Commission. The facts are as
follows:

Director Waltz was employed by the Fallbrook Fire Protection
District on May 17, 1972 as a call-man. At the time,
Director Waltz was and still is an active member and on
active duty in the U.S. Marine Corps. A call-man for the
Fire protection District is essentially a reservist who is
called and assists the fire department of Fallbrook on a
volunteer basis. Call-men, however, are paid for their
services when called. By public election on November 28, 1975,

SACHSE & JAMES
ATTORNEYS AT LAW

Fair Political Practices Commission, cont.                Page 2.

Director Waltz was elected as a member of the Board of
Directors of the Fallbrook Fire Protection District. Since
his election, he has continued to act as a call-man for the
Fire Department and continues to receive remuneration for
his services. During 1975 he earned $1,904.00, said sum
was paid to him by the Fallbrook Fire Protection District.

During the course of his duties as a Director, Mr. Waltz is
called upon to make decisions regarding the call-men section
of the Fire Department including the organization and
structure of the call-men section, the pay scale of the call-
men section, and the potential hiring of call-men as regular
fire department members. Since his election, Director Waltz
has participated in all discussions of the Board concerning
call-men. However, upon my advice, he has refrained from
voting on any matter relating to the call-men section.

Director Waltz's concern and the Board of Director's concern
is that his position as a member of the Board of Directors
may be in conflict with his acting as a call-man to such an
extent that a mere abstention from voting may not be suffi-
cient to protect him under the current law. As such, this
request for a formal opinion is made hereby.

If any information is required, please do not hesitate to
contact this office.

Thank you in advance for your cooperation.

Sincerely,

SACHSE & JAMES

Robert H. James

RHJ/nk
